pdf_context: You can trace the origin of this love story back one hundred years exactly, to the founding of Route 66.

The Dust Bowl was a ten-year environmental disaster that struck the Great Plains, in the middle of the country.
So when a severe drought hit in the early 1930s, massive windstorms blew the loose topsoil all over the region, covering towns and farms in thick dust.

The Dust Bowl coincided with an economic depression.

After World War II, Americans had the wealth to own their own cars, and the family road trip became a popular option for middle-class vacations.
Route 66 transformed itself into a linear tourist destination, with neon signs, roadside attractions, campgrounds, and family-friendly motels with swimming pools.

Unlike other contractions, ma’am doesn’t connect or combine two words—it only shortens one word.
“Ma’am” is a formal way to address a woman.
It’s the female equivalent of the word “sir.” This can often be considered polite and respectful, but it always implies an emotional distance between two people.

A police office might say, “Step out of the car please, ma’am.” A customer service agent might say, “Can I help you, ma’am?” But I would caution you against using “ma’am” unless you’re sure it’s appropriate.

The origin of “o’clock” goes back to the early days of mechanical timekeeping.

Most languages simply say “hour,” translated literally “it is one hour” or “they are two hours.” Or simply, “it is two” or “it is one.” Only English, Welsh, and Gaelic use a term like “o’clock” in timekeeping.
Before mechanical clocks, people kept time with sundials, hourglasses, and church bells—all of which were imprecise.
